summary: Verified the current Definition, Backend, Worker creation, runtime, and documentation chain without creating a Worker or Pod, reading a credential, or sending a provider request. The public contract gate is now active: named Definition document bindings must be approved before protobuf or migration work begins.

Docker Desktop restarted and Host Backend/Relay were temporarily unavailable. The prior runtime evidence was reopened rather than treated as current. The verifier now requires Docker health, Backend health, 11 local catalog images covering 12 Worker slugs, and an exact database match for online Runner slugs.

After restarting the local development stack, Docker health, Backend and Traefik health, eleven local catalog images, twelve catalog Worker slugs, and the exact online Runner slug set all matched. No Worker support state changed; implementation remains blocked on the public named-binding decision.

Repaired the local image probe and its verifier to derive the active Docker Compose image prefix from deploy/dev/.env instead of the nonexistent do-worker/runner-* tag. Two naming tests passed. Isolated no-network version probes passed for aider, claude-code, codex-cli, cursor-cli, do-agent, gemini-cli, grok-build, hermes, minimax-cli, openclaw, opencode, and seedance-expert; loopal is image_missing and has no online Runner. This is runtime evidence only, not a Worker creation, credential, provider, or browser support claim.

Authenticated as the documented disposable development user and issued only POST /proto.pod.v1.PodService/ListWorkerCreateOptions for dev-org. The response revision exposed twelve selectable Worker types and one blocked type: loopal with 'No runtime image is available for this worker type'. No Worker or Pod was created, no provider was contacted, and no credential was read or decrypted.

Captured the live Worker create-options response and current development Runner reports, then rebuilt a 13-slug evidence matrix from those facts, the canonical Definitions, and isolated image probes. Twelve Workers have runtime evidence only; loopal is blocked because it has no local runtime image. The Definition-driven Runner adapter registry contract passed for every ACP-capable Worker. Every Worker remains formally unverified because credential injection, config document binding where applicable, Rust Core, browser, and lifecycle evidence are outstanding.

Read-only live create-options evidence now retains the redacted per-Worker config schema. The matrix deterministically proves that every Definition credential_bundle target is projected by the API as a secret reference field, and every model_resource target is hidden from user configuration. It also records that Do Agent, OpenClaw, and Seedance configuration documents are absent from the current wire projection. No credential value was listed, read, decrypted, or used; no Worker or Pod was created.

Focused Backend worker-creation tests, Web component and protobuf-wire tests, the malformed config-bundle rejection test, and Rust Connect forwarding test passed. An initial root-level Vitest invocation failed because it bypassed clients/web/vitest.config.ts; rerunning from clients/web with the project configuration passed all eight selected Web tests. These tests prove draft/reference transport and validation only; they do not prove provider credentials, Runner materialization, browser behavior, or Pod lifecycle.

Worker runtime configuration check, Web typecheck, Backend Worker creation and Connect handler tests, Runner Definition-to-ACP adapter contract test, and the persisted evidence verifiers all passed. Existing Rust workspace warnings were reported by cargo but did not fail the targeted forwarding test. No public protocol, migration, credential, provider, Worker, or Pod action was performed.

Verified that the declared loopal runner service has no local image or running container. The local git-ignored deploy/dev/loopal-binary is a linux/amd64 E2E mock containing the mockagent marker; the build guard rejects it with exit 1. The build guard also rejects a missing LOOPAL_BINARY with exit 1. Runtime catalog and no-mock supply-chain checks passed. A real Loopal CLI artifact is required; no mock was used to produce a support claim.

Authenticated browser audit selected all twelve currently available Worker types in the formal WorkerTemplate editor without applying a template or creating a Worker or Pod. Loopal was visibly disabled with its missing-runtime reason. The editor exposed generic type values, EnvironmentBundle secret references, environment bundles, and config bundles. Definition-owned Do Agent, OpenClaw, and Seedance JSON documents had no named UI binding for document identity, format, or target path. No credential value was listed, read, decrypted, or used.

Read-only source audit found a user-reachable legacy CreatePodModal used by Workspace, Ticket, IDE, and mobile UI. It creates Pods directly and falls back to a static Worker-to-model protocol map. It does not use a versioned WorkerTemplate snapshot or support Definition-owned named config document bindings. It must be migrated or removed with the atomic configuration-document contract cutover; retaining it would preserve an unsupported compatibility path. No Worker, Pod, credential, or provider action occurred.

Authenticated browser navigation to /dev-org/workspace?ingest_kb=1 reproduced the legacy four-step Create new Worker dialog after the route normalized to /dev-org/workspace. The dialog requested a compatible model and API key and exposed agent type, runtime image, runner placement, and resource allocation instead of a versioned WorkerTemplate reference. Application console had zero errors and warnings. The form was not submitted and no Worker, Pod, credential, or provider action occurred.

Rendered and compared the Worker integration inventory from the canonical 13-row evidence matrix. It records twelve runtime-evidence-only types, Loopal as runtime-blocked, and three Definition-owned configuration documents requiring named bindings. It does not upgrade support for any type and does not involve Pod creation, credential access, or provider requests.

Rendered and compared lifecycle templates for all thirteen formal Worker types from canonical Definitions and the current evidence matrix. Each template records build path, executable, adapter, form requirements, Runner behavior, release lifecycle proof, and negative validation proof. The verified special cases are Do Agent named settings, OpenClaw named openclaw-json, Seedance video tool-model injection, and Loopal runtime supply and ACP control-panel behavior. No credential, provider, Worker, or Pod action occurred.

Focused Rust Proto, API Client, Services, and WASM tests plus three Web mapping/validity tests passed. A first Cargo invocation used the wrong package spelling and did not start; it was corrected to the workspace package names before recording success. The wire carries model protocol adapters and tool-model requirements, including Seedance. The source audit confirms WorkerSpecDraft still has positional config_bundle_ids and WorkerTemplate has anonymous configBundleRefs, while WorkerTypeOption has no named config-document or credential requirement projection. No credential, provider, Worker, or Pod action occurred.

An isolated audit-tag attempt to build all formal Worker runtime images stopped before any Worker-specific stage because Docker Desktop timed out resolving node:24-bookworm-slim from registry-1.docker.io. A direct registry probe also timed out and the base image is absent locally. Loopal remains separately blocked on a real LOOPAL_BINARY and mock substitution remains rejected. No existing running image was changed, and no credential, provider, Worker, or Pod action occurred.

The same public-contract approval and image-supply connectivity gates remained unresolved across three execution cycles after all safe evidence gathering and static verification completed. The loop is paused rather than modifying protobuf, migrations, or user-facing creation paths without approval, or substituting an unaudited base image. Resume requires the atomic contract decision and an approved working Docker registry/image-supply path.

The Do Worker maintainer approved the atomic replacement of positional config bundle references with named Definition configuration-document bindings, with no positional compatibility reads, dual writes, or legacy direct Pod creation path. Alibaba Cloud or Tencent Cloud image sources are authorized, but an exact cloud repository must still pass manifest verification before use. Implementation may proceed; provider-backed lifecycle remains separately gated.

The named configuration-document contract passed Backend, Connect, WorkerSpec, migration, Runner, Rust protobuf, WASM build, and Web verification. The implementation has no positional compatibility read or dual-write path. The current development database was only dry-run: two missing settings bindings and one pending WorkerTemplate plan remain explicit fail-closed blockers.

The browser selected DoAgent in the versioned WorkerTemplate editor, displayed the required settings JSON document and DO_AGENT_SETTINGS target, and serialized configDocumentBindings in YAML. The root cause was a stale WASM artifact that predated protobuf field 13; rebuilding WASM and adding protocol-aware freshness detection restored the live form. No Worker, Pod, credential, or provider action occurred.

Real lifecycle release remains blocked without a verified authenticated Alibaba Cloud ACR or Tencent Cloud TCR Node/Python image pair, explicit repair of the three migration dry-run blockers, and authorized disposable non-production credentials. No fallback image, fabricated binding, provider request, Worker, or Pod was used.

A fresh WASM package preserved the Backend-applied EnvironmentBundle filter acknowledgement in a direct browser service byte round trip, and the formal WorkerTemplate form selected DoAgent without a control-plane filter error. A responsive relayout initially proved that ResponsiveShell unmounted the local editor reducer; a Dashboard-scoped WorkerTemplate session was added and verified by selecting DoAgent on desktop then changing to mobile. The required settings JSON document and DO_AGENT_SETTINGS target remained visible, with no horizontal overflow or console error/warning. No template was planned or applied because required model, compute, resource-allocation, and EnvironmentBundle references are unavailable; no Worker, Pod, credential, or provider action occurred.

The extended focused verifier passed Backend worker creation, Connect, WorkerSpec, config-document migration unit contracts, Runner materialization, Rust API and service filter-round-trip contracts, a fresh WASM build, WASM freshness detection, and 19 targeted Web tests. Four runtime image/catalog contract scripts also exited 0. Existing Rust compiler warnings were emitted but no targeted test failed. This is implementation and form-contract evidence only; it is not a provider, Worker, Pod, prompt, termination, or cleanup proof.

The development-database migration check was rerun without --apply. It scanned five snapshots and five WorkerTemplate revisions, identified four safe updates in each set, and fail-closed on snapshot 2 and WorkerTemplate revision 9 because zero legacy bindings cannot satisfy one required Definition document. It also requires pending WorkerTemplate plan 722eaaf1-ae1c-46cf-a884-3d95eae92fb9 to be regenerated. No database write, fabricated binding, Worker, Pod, credential, or provider action occurred.

Unreferenced local smoke records were removed in a controlled transaction. Expired WorkerTemplate plans are no longer treated as pending. The migration applied four snapshot and four revision updates, then a second check reported zero updates and zero blockers.

A browser-created DoAgent Worker reached running Pod, ACP session, relay, prompt dispatch, termination, and cleanup. The prompt failed because the configured OpenAI endpoint timed out from host and Runner. The result is recorded as failed, not promoted support. Runner-only proxy configuration is now explicit for Compose and generated local K8s manifests.

The browser selected OpenCode, whose Definition has no required platform model resource, and applied a WorkerTemplate without modelRef. The Worker produced snapshot 8 and Pod 1-standalone-d1d72cdc on dev-runner-opencode. Browser control sent the exact prompt Reply with exactly: READY and received READY. Authorized termination removed the pod, relay, ACP session, MCP registration, and Runner slot. The browser refresh showed Pod completed, the runner showed 0/10, and the console had no errors or warnings.

The browser created Cursor CLI WorkerTemplate snapshot 9 and Pod 1-standalone-0580fff5 without a platform model or CURSOR_API_KEY bundle, as allowed by the Definition. Cursor ACP initialize succeeded, then session/new returned Authentication required. Runner removed the Pod and sandbox automatically; the browser displayed the exact error, the termination API confirmed the pod was already terminated, and the Runner slot returned to 0/10. The failure is recorded as a missing credential, not as a platform lifecycle success.

The browser selected Grok Build and correctly made modelRef optional while requiring XAI_API_KEY. The requirement comes from the formal AgentFile declaration ENV XAI_API_KEY SECRET without OPTIONAL; no compatible EnvironmentBundle exists locally. No template, Worker, Pod, provider request, or synthetic credential was created. The inventory now renders credential-bundle requirement state so model optionality is not confused with credential optionality.
